step_by_step_reasoning: We identify the main actor as UserConsumerMobile representing the rider and driver apps. Route 53 handles DNS and geolocation routing. ELB distributes traffic to either the legacy Nomad cluster (EC2) or the new EKS cluster. Both compute clusters access dedicated RDS MySQL databases for active data. Cold trip data is eventually archived from the microservices to S3.

graph.name: Cabify Mobile Platform Architecture
graph.notes: Cabify uses this architecture to run real-time ride-sharing and delivery applications, migrating from a Nomad cluster on EC2 to Amazon EKS while utilizing Spot Instances for cost efficiency.

nodes.name: Mobile Client App
nodes.notes: Mobile apps used by riders and drivers.

nodes.name: Amazon Route 53
nodes.notes: Handles IP geolocation routing during platform migration.

nodes.name: Nomad Cluster
nodes.notes: Legacy container orchestration platform running on EC2.

nodes.name: Amazon EKS Cluster
nodes.notes: Target Kubernetes cluster running containerized microservices.

nodes.name: Amazon RDS MySQL
nodes.notes: Independent databases owned by individual microservices.

nodes.name: Amazon S3
nodes.notes: Long-term cold storage for completed trip data.

edges.notes: Resolves domain via Route 53 with geolocation.
